page: Vultr vx1-m-16c-128g-960s is a memory-optimized virtual server belonging to the VX1 Cloud Compute family. It features 16 shared vCPUs powered by an AMD EPYC-Turin processor running at 2.0 GHz, paired with 128.0 GB of system memory. This configuration provides a high memory-to-core ratio of 8.0 GB per vCPU. Storage is supported by a local 960 GB NVMe SSD, and the instance includes one complimentary public IPv4 address.

Performance benchmarks reveal a contrast between single-core and multi-core capabilities. Single-core stress-ng CPU performance and single-threaded compression tasks rank in the top tier, placing in the top 10% of tested systems. However, multi-core CPU performance and multi-threaded decompression fall into the average tier. Memory performance is strong, with Passmark memory scores in the top 25%, while large-block memory bandwidth remains average. For lightweight AI workloads, the server demonstrates top-tier text generation speeds on small 135M models, though performance drops to average on larger 7B models.

Given the shared CPU allocation, this server offers a cost-efficient profile for memory-intensive workloads that do not require continuous, dedicated multi-core compute. The combination of 128.0 GB RAM and fast NVMe storage makes it well-suited for database caching, memory-mapped file processing, lightweight LLM inference, and web serving applications.

This configuration yields a high resource density of 8.0 GB of memory per core, complemented by a 960 GB local NVMe SSD. Benchmark data highlights top-tier single-core CPU performance and single-threaded compression speeds, alongside strong memory performance and efficient small-model LLM text generation. However, multi-core processing and large-block memory bandwidth remain average due to the shared CPU allocation. This resource profile provides a cost-efficient solution for memory-heavy workloads that do not demand sustained, dedicated multi-core execution, making it suitable for database caching, web serving, and lightweight machine learning inference.
